constructs.definition: Quantitative chauvinism is a style of reasoning that privileges quantitative metrics as having jurisdiction over political judgments, collapses heterogeneous social complexity into a single aggregated trajectory, and then inscribes the result in portable artifacts that travel as if they were neutral facts — the use of numerical abstractions to close political questions before they can be opened, to present simplifications as comprehensiveness, and to frame dissent as a failure to understand the data. It extends Theodore Porter's account of mechanical objectivity as a "technology of trust" by specifying the three orientations — the will to simplify, the rule of expertise, and the politics of inevitability — through which that technology operates in demographic governance. Quantitative chauvinism provides the epistemic infrastructure that lebensraum imaginaries require: without simplified models that suppress feedback and alternative trajectories, naturalizing demographic hierarchy loses its causal warrant.

Lebensraum imaginaries name a recurring epistemic architecture within ecofascism that describes an ensemble of future narratives, organizational practices, and rhetorical repertoires through which ecological emergency is converted into demographic hierarchy and coercive governance is presented as reluctant but responsible management of natural limits. An imaginary is a collectively held structure that makes certain actions seem necessary, certain populations seem threatening, and certain policies seem like responsible concessions, even as individuals, organizations, data, and arguments enter and exit while the structure persists and adapts. "Lebensraum" is a deliberate reference to a concept crossing biological, ecological, social, and political boundaries that animated the calamities of the Third Reich but has a longer history and more unsettling afterlives. The construct extends Roger Griffin's account of palingenetic ultranationalism by specifying how ecological crisis supplies the rebirth myth and quantitative instruments supply the epistemic infrastructure; the book uses lebensraum imaginaries for the recurring architecture and reserves "ecofascism" for the verdict on individuals.

chapters.title: Preface
chapters.teaser: Not a verdict on individuals but a tracing of the structures that recruit them.
chapters.pitch: An STS reckoning with how quantitative authority gets captured for reactionary ends, tracing Albert Bartlett's celebrated exponential-growth lecture as it travels from the physics classroom into ecofascist immigration politics. It names the two co-evolving mechanisms — quantitative chauvinism and lebensraum imaginaries — that convert numbers into moral permission for coercion.
chapters.summary: Written by a computational social scientist returning to his STS roots, this book reclaims quantitative methods from reactionary projects that have, for centuries, abused numerical data to advance cruel visions for protecting the future from the present. It reads the contemporary polycrisis of democratic backsliding, climate crisis, and technological disruption through the frame of ecofascism — the fusion of ecological emergency with racialized demographic hierarchy — and traces Albert Bartlett's lecture (1,742 performances across four decades) as it travels from the lecture hall to the advisory boards of immigration-restriction organizations. The book gives this analysis two names: lebensraum imaginaries, a recurring epistemic architecture within ecofascism, and quantitative chauvinism, the epistemic infrastructure that imaginaries require. Reserving "ecofascism" as a verdict rather than a slur, it asks how immunological metaphors like inoculation might teach anticipatory recognition, and closes by proposing quantitative egalitarianism.

chapters.title: Boulder
chapters.teaser: A progressive city builds the most elaborate growth-control machinery in America — and ends up whiter than the state around it.
chapters.pitch: Boulder introduces the book's two operating concepts through a century of its own institutional history, showing how conservation arithmetic slid into demographic exclusion one 'neutral' threshold at a time.
chapters.summary: Boulder, Colorado — an alluvial fan beneath the Flatirons settled by miners in 1858 after Arapaho and Cheyenne dispossession — becomes the book's most legible proving ground for how a community converts anxieties about disorder into 'objective' numerical analysis. Keegan traces an institutional plumbing of bounded-endowment, selective-access, and expert-stewardship logic: the Colorado Chautauqua (1898), Frederick Law Olmsted Jr.'s 1910 'city of homes' report, a Klan klavern and its Rocky Mountain American paper (1922–25), Saco DeBoer's 1928 zoning code after Euclid v. Ambler, then physicist Albert Bartlett and Bob McKelvey's PLAN-Boulder, the 1959 Blue Line, the 1967 open space program, the Danish Plan's 450-permit cap, and the 1977 Comprehensive Plan's population ceiling. The result, by 2020, was a city 83 percent non-Hispanic white in a 66-percent state. The chapter names two co-evolving mechanisms — quantitative chauvinism (extending Theodore Porter) and lebensraum imaginaries (extending Roger Griffin) — and notes Bartlett's FAIR, Carrying Capacity Network, and Tanton-network affiliations that recast his arithmetic as exclusion.

chapters.title: The Inheritance
chapters.teaser: Before Bartlett ever picked up the chalk, the apparatus, the vocabulary, and the audience were already in place.
chapters.pitch: Bartlett's 1969 lecture invented nothing. This chapter traces the four currents — eugenics, Lebensraum, conservation, and the postwar population establishment — that built the world his arithmetic landed in.
chapters.summary: The chapter argues that Albert Bartlett's September 1969 Boulder lecture inherited a ready-made apparatus, vocabulary, and audience assembled across the first half of the twentieth century through four converging currents. The first is the institutional eugenics apparatus Charles Davenport and Harry Laughlin ran at the Eugenics Record Office at Cold Spring Harbor (1910–1940), codified through sterilization law, ratified in Buck v. Bell (1927), and laundered into demography via the Population Association of America (1931). The second is the German Lebensraum architecture from Friedrich Ratzel (1897) through Rudolf Kjellén to Karl Haushofer. The third is the American conservation movement, sharing donors and personnel — Madison Grant, Henry Fairfield Osborn, the Boone and Crockett Club — and resting on Manifest Destiny's dispossession of Indigenous nations. The fourth is the postwar population-concern community: the Population Council (1952), William Vogt, Paul Ehrlich, Garrett Hardin, the Club of Rome, and Zero Population Growth. The named mechanisms are quantitative chauvinism and lebensraum imaginaries — durable because embedded, not consciously endorsed.

chapters.title: The Bottle
chapters.teaser: He gave the same lecture 1,742 times. Each time, arithmetic quietly became obligation.
chapters.pitch: How a Manhattan Project physicist's doubling-time trick turned exponential arithmetic into environmental common sense — and a warrant for controlling who may live where.
chapters.summary: Between 1968 and 1994 the populationist concern community turned exponential arithmetic into environmental "common sense." Albert Bartlett, a Harvard-trained Los Alamos physicist who co-founded PLAN-Boulder, delivered "Arithmetic, Population, and Energy" 1,742 times from 1969, using the law of 70 (divide seventy by the growth rate for doubling time) and a bacteria-in-a-bottle thought experiment. The chapter names the mechanism — domesticated scientism — whereby an expert claim, stripped of its methodological caveats through a "tutorial register," is re-presented as civic fact. Replaying Bartlett's constant 2 percent rate overshoots the actual 2025 U.S. population (347 million) by the size of Indonesia; the logistic and Gompertz curves and Notestein's demographic transition, all available by 1969, landed near the mark. Drawing on Porter, Desrosières, and Venuti, the chapter shows arithmetic naturalizing a lebensraum imaginary through "carrying capacity." The 1995 Population and Environment exchange with Gretchen Daily and the Ehrlichs — and Bartlett's phrase "the carrying capacity of our culture" — marks the math becoming explicit immigration argument.

chapters.title: The Quarantine
chapters.teaser: After Cairo, the movement lost its license — so it built a new one out of footnotes.
chapters.pitch: When the 1994 Cairo conference delegitimized population control, John Tanton built a parallel credentialing network that laundered nativist conclusions through fiscal-impact tables and the aesthetics of neutral expertise.
chapters.summary: When the September 1994 Cairo International Conference on Population and Development replaced population control with reproductive autonomy, the population-concern community's residual conviction migrated into U.S. immigration restriction. John Tanton, a Petoskey ophthalmologist and former Sierra Club population-committee chair, built a parallel credentialing apparatus between 1979 and 2008 — FAIR, the Center for Immigration Studies, NumbersUSA, U.S. English, and The Social Contract Press — funded by the Colcom and Pioneer Funds. Its signature instrument was the immigration impact statement: Donald Huddle's 1993 study claiming immigrants cost $42.5 billion a year, built by cross-tabulating costs while omitting taxes paid. The chapter names the resulting mechanisms — the residual lever, misaggregation, the rule of expertise, colorblind racism, and hierarchization — and calls the network's durable strategy agnotological infrastructuring: manufacturing strategic ignorance through self-citing institutions. Beneath the fiscal vocabulary ran the lebensraum imaginary. The boundary held until the Census Bureau's August 2008 majority-minority-by-2042 projection bypassed the laundering machinery entirely.

chapters.title: The Swarm
chapters.teaser: A federal press release became one of the most-cited numbers in a mass-shooter's manifesto.
chapters.pitch: How the Census Bureau's 2008 majority-minority crossover chart was decoupled from its makers, stripped of its caveats, and recruited as the epistemic backbone of replacement politics. The chapter names the operation: demographic engineering.
chapters.summary: On August 14, 2008, the Census Bureau's Population Division released NP2008, a cohort-component projection showing the non-Hispanic-white share crossing below fifty percent in 2042. Its crossover chart, two converging lines on a shared axis, rendered a conditional scenario as a countdown. The chapter shows the finding was an artifact of three classificatory choices encoded in OMB Directive 15: defining 'non-Hispanic white alone' narrowly, treating Hispanic as a cross-cutting ethnicity, and coding multiracial people as minority. Drawing on Porter, Desrosières, Bowker and Star, Alba, Levy, and Myers, it traces how the projection, decoupled from its federal makers, traveled through Brookings, the Tanton network, Tucker Carlson, and chan-boards into the El Paso (2019) and Buffalo (2022) manifestos, its conditionality stripped at every stage. The 2020 census's 276 percent multiracial surge falsified the model's stability assumption; the Bureau abandoned majority-minority projections in 2023. The chapter names this operation demographic engineering, reads the chart as financial risk-tranching, and ends with the 2024 mifepristone complaint inverting the logic toward pronatalism.

chapters.title: The Portfolio
chapters.teaser: Overpopulation panic flips into its mirror image: where did all the babies go?
chapters.pitch: Pronatalism recruits the very infrastructure that once justified restriction: cost-per-immigrant becomes return-per-birth, wombs become policy levers, and zero population growth and pronatalism turn out to be the same project.
chapters.summary: Opening with the winter 2024 mifepristone complaint whose appendix of fertility tables priced each birth as a "demographic return on investment" and each prevented birth as a fiscal loss, the chapter argues the Portfolio era inverts demographic anxiety's sign while preserving its structure. Building on Dobbs (2022), it shows restriction and pronatalism as structurally identical optimizations: Viktor Orbán's 2019 Hungarian "demographic emergency" loan-forgiveness and tax-exemption scheme, Poland's 500+, Russian maternal capital, and a cross-partisan coalition all convert reproduction into a fiscal variable. It names two mechanisms. Fine-tuning imports financial-portfolio optimization (objective functions, the discount rate) into demographic governance, burying political choices in technical parameters. Temporal gerrymandering selects projection horizons to manufacture urgency. Stress tests, using Huntington-Hill apportionment and cohort-component sensitivity, show abortion restriction (~70,000 births/year) is a far weaker representation lever than the immigration cuts the same coalition pursues (~800,000/year). Throughout, quantitative chauvinism, lebensraum imaginaries, and TESCREAL longtermism recur.

chapters.title: The Evacuation
chapters.teaser: When the bottle is finally full, the question stops being who stays and becomes who gets rescued.
chapters.pitch: A speculative near-future era where climate-migration shock-spillover models are handed to an autonomous AI resettlement authority, and a bioregional vocabulary of regenerative sovereignty becomes the progressive idiom in which Garrett Hardin's lifeboat ethics returns.
chapters.summary: Opening on the IPCC AR6 Working Group II human-mobility chapter (2022), the chapter traces how climate-induced migration became a modeling object rooted in The Limits to Growth (1972), Homer-Dixon's environmental-conflict thesis, the World Bank's Groundswell reports, and the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways. These converge into a shock-spillover framework, advanced by the Cascade Institute, the Potsdam Institute, and the Stockholm Resilience Centre, that relocates the political question from the displaced person's claim to the receiving region's absorption capacity. Applying the book's diagnostics of quantitative chauvinism (will to simplify, rule of expertise, politics of inevitability) and lebensraum imaginaries, it projects a speculative Adaptive Resettlement Coordination System, an autonomous AI decision system, that sorts the displaced into pathways (Active Stewardship, Managed Transition, Phased Reduction, Memorial Documentation). Drawing on Elish's moral crumple zone, longtermism/TESCREAL, overshoot, and ecobordering, it argues this regenerative-sovereignty regime is Hardin's 1974 lifeboat ethics with the lifeboat replaced by the bioregion, where ecofascism is earned.

chapters.title: The Long Term
chapters.teaser: The future has been ceded to people who use it as a warrant for present cruelty. Take it back.
chapters.pitch: Long-term thinking has been ceded to longtermisms that treat the future as a warrant for present coercion. This chapter reclaims it as intersectional longtermism, drawn from five traditions that have already survived projects of erasure.
chapters.summary: Because the operations the book tracks are intergenerational projects that think in centuries, the contest must be fought on the long term, which is 'already occupied' by longtermisms that convert the future into a single quantity licensing present coercion: the effective altruism of MacAskill and Ord and the TESCREAL bundle Gebru and Torres trace to eugenics; the technocratic discounting of Stern versus Nordhaus and Malm and Carton's overshoot; the captured regenerative sovereignty of Chapter 7; the abstract civic deep-time of the Long Now; and doomerist collapse science like Turchin's cliodynamics. Against them the chapter names intersectional longtermism, treating the future as plural and the present as a site of choice, drawn from five traditions of surviving and prevailing: Black ecologies and abolition, Indigenous continuance, disability justice, reproductive justice, and antifascist-cooperative organizing. From these it distills a program of quantitative egalitarianism, which demotes the number from verdict to evidence, and solidarity infrastructuring, carrying these commitments into the long heat.

chapters.title: Boulder Again
chapters.teaser: What the model strips from bacteria — memory, feedback, survival — is what lebensraum imaginaries strip from us.
chapters.pitch: The book returns to Boulder, where the arithmetic of concern was first taught, and reads Bartlett's bacteria-in-a-bottle as the same analytical mistake at two scales: the question is no longer who belongs, but what the valley owes the people the long heat will send.
chapters.summary: Returning to where it opened, the chapter pairs Albert Bartlett's two 2013 obituaries — the Daily Camera's beloved physicist versus The Social Contract's restrictionist ally — to read his "bacteria in a bottle" as a micro-macro isomorphism. Real bacteria possess feedback, coordination, memory, and survival (quorum sensing, CRISPR-Cas immune memory, persister cells, horizontal gene transfer); Bartlett's bottle strips these out exactly as Lebensraum imaginaries strip them from human communities. Keegan provocatively pairs Chapter 8's five resistance traditions with biological mechanisms, then guards against biological determinism, organicism, and metaphorical drift. Grounding the argument in Boulder, he traces the Cheyenne and Arapaho as recent recombined, post-pandemic Plains arrivals, and the 1864 Sand Creek Massacre under Chivington as the local precedent for what the architecture produces under climatic stress. A deep-time coda — Flatirons, Pliocene and Miocene analogs, pika, cottonwoods, the 2021 Marshall Fire — recasts carrying capacity as the wrong question, replacing "who belongs" with what the valley owes its arrivals.
